summary: Recommendation: (b) use SAM3 narrowly — do NOT replace the tracker. SAM3 video tracking fails two of your three hard gates: (1) cost — measured 5-6 fps on an H200 at 1080p (Meta's own paper says real-time for ~28 objects needs 4x H200); on a T4 with ~25 concurrent masklets you're looking at roughly 1-3 fps, i.e. ~12-40 GPU-h per 90-min match vs your 3.5 GPU-h gate and current 1.2 GPU-h. (2) identity — the SAM3 tracker matches propagated masklets to new detections by spatio-temporal IoU, not appearance; a player who leaves frame for 30s+ under a panning camera comes back as a NEW masklet ("new masklets are created when new objects appear"), and Meta's paper concedes "the merging might suffer from ambiguities, especially in crowded scenes." It would reproduce, not fix, your 8,695-tracklet fragmentation. The license gate it passes: the custom "SAM License" (not Apache-2.0 like SAM1/SAM2, but not GPL/AGPL) explicitly permits commercial use of code+weights. The genuinely high-ROI move: run SAM3 in IMAGE mode (30 ms/frame for 100+ objects on H200, so ~0.3-0.5 s/frame on T4) on sampled keyframes to (a) produce grass-free, mask-cleaned player crops for your SigLIP team embeddings — the most plausible lever to push your 0.479 silhouette up and improve tracklet merging — and (b) replace the HSV pitch heuristic with prompted pitch segmentation at ~1 frame/2s. Both fit inside ~0.5-0.8 added GPU-h (total ~2.0h, under the gate). Skip jersey-number exemplar prompting — far-side numbers are a few pixels, below SAM3's useful concept-detection scale. One operational caution: weights are gated on HuggingFace (accept terms, share contact info), and avoid the Ultralytics SAM3 wrapper in the serving path (Ultralytics code is AGPL-3.0).
license: VERIFIED from the raw LICENSE file in facebookresearch/sam3 (github.com/facebookresearch/sam3/blob/main/LICENSE): it is NOT Apache-2.0 — it is a custom "SAM License" covering BOTH code and weights: "'SAM Materials' means models, software and algorithms, including machine-learning model code, trained model weights, inference-enabling code, training-enabling code, fine-tuning enabling code." Grant: "a non-exclusive, worldwide, non-transferable and royalty-free limited license" to "use, reproduce, distribute, copy, create derivative works of, and make modifications to the SAM Materials." Commercial SaaS use: YES, permitted (no revenue caps, no non-commercial clause). Restrictions: (1) field-of-use ban — no ITAR/Trade-Controls end uses, "military or warfare purposes, nuclear industries or applications, espionage, or the development or use of guns or illegal weapons" (football analytics is unaffected); (2) redistribution of materials/derivatives must carry the SAM License; (3) no reverse engineering of SAM Materials; (4) research-publication acknowledgment; (5) IP-litigation termination clause; (6) indemnification of Meta. Notably ABSENT: any restriction on using outputs/masks to train other models (so mask-cleaned crops can feed your own models freely). HuggingFace model card (facebook/sam3): license tag "other", access GATED — "You need to agree to share your contact information to access this model." Meta's announcement confirms: "We're sharing SAM 3 under the SAM License." Bottom line for your constraint: not OSI-approved open source (field-of-use restrictions), but contains no GPL/AGPL/copyleft terms — fine for a commercial serving path. Caveat: do not pull SAM3 in via Ultralytics (docs.ultralytics.com/models/sam-3) — Ultralytics' wrapper code is AGPL-3.0; use Meta's repo or HF Transformers integration.
capabilities: SAM3 (released Nov 19, 2025; 848M params, "0.9B" on HF) = a DETR-based open-vocabulary DETECTOR (conditioned on text noun-phrases, boxes/points, and image exemplars) + a SAM2-style memory TRACKER sharing one vision encoder. New vs SAM2: Promptable Concept Segmentation (PCS) — prompt "player" once and it finds/segments ALL instances in image or video (SAM2 required a manual prompt per object instance); roughly 2x cgF1 over prior open-vocab segmentation on the new SA-Co benchmark. Video: detector output spawns "masklets" (spatio-temporal masks); each frame, propagated masklets are matched to fresh detections by IoU, a "masklet detection score" over a temporal window suppresses unreliable tracks, and the tracker "periodically re-prompts itself with high-confidence detection masks" to recover from drift/short occlusion. THE CRITICAL ANSWER ON IDENTITY: matching is spatio-temporal IoU only — there is NO appearance-based or global re-identification. The paper states unmatched detections create new masklets ("new masklets are created when new objects appear") and that during inference "we only retain frames where the object is confidently present in the memory bank." A player gone for 30+ seconds under a panning camera (frame content completely changed) will have their masklet suppressed/terminated and will be RE-DETECTED AS A NEW INSTANCE on re-entry. Meta explicitly flags the weak spot: "The merging might suffer from ambiguities, especially in crowded scenes." SAM3 handles short occlusions (seconds, object near last position) better than SAM2, but your 30s+ out-of-frame case is architecturally unsolved — you would still need SigLIP-style appearance merging on top. SAM 3.1 (blogged at ai.meta.com, ~Apr 2026) adds "object multiplexing" (up to 16 objects per forward pass, shared-memory joint tracking) and global reasoning — a speed/VRAM fix, with no claimed long-gap re-ID improvement.
throughput: Reported numbers: IMAGE mode — "On an H200 GPU, SAM 3 runs in 30 ms for a single image with 100+ detected objects" (paper). VIDEO mode — "inference latency scales with the number of objects, sustaining near real-time performance for ~5 concurrent objects" on one H200; the paper's own recipe for 30-fps real-time is multi-GPU: 10 objects = 2x H200, 28 objects = 4x H200, 64 objects = 8x H200. Independent measurement (GitHub issue #425): 5-6 fps on a single H200 at 1080p video propagation vs 30+ fps for SAM2 on the same hardware (codebase doesn't cleanly expose fp16/bf16; some float32 casts). SAM 3.1: 16 -> 32 fps on a single H100, multiplexing up to 16 objects per pass; community coverage reports ~8GB -> ~4GB VRAM at FP16. Memory: video predictor inference state grows with frames/objects — documented OOMs on the example notebook (issue #169); mitigations are offload_video_to_cpu / async frame loading, meaning long videos must be chunked, which itself fragments identity at chunk boundaries. T4 ESTIMATE (honest): a T4 (Turing, 16GB, ~65 TFLOPS FP16, no bf16, no FlashAttention) is ~8-15x slower than H100/H200 for transformer inference. With ~25 concurrent masklets (22 players + refs), expect ~0.5-1.5 fps with SAM3, optimistically 2-4 fps with SAM 3.1 multiplexing (25 objects needs 2 multiplex groups). A 90-min match at 25 fps = 135,000 frames: at 3 fps that's ~12.5 GPU-h; at 1 fps, ~37.5 GPU-h. VERDICT: blows your 3.5 GPU-h gate by ~4-10x minimum (vs current 1.2 GPU-h), and even on a rented H100 it's ~2-2.5 H100-h (~$6-10/match at 6-10x your current $1) while still not solving identity. IMAGE-mode-only usage is a different story: ~0.3-0.5 s/frame on T4; sampling 1 fps (5,400 frames/match) adds ~0.5-0.8 GPU-h — fits your gate with headroom.
sports_fit: No published case of SAM2/SAM3 doing full-match, 22-player tracking at production scale; the evidence points the other way. (1) The most on-point study, "Team-Aware Football Player Tracking with SAM" (arXiv 2512.08467, Dec 2025), reports SAM-based football tracking at 7.6-7.7 fps with 100% success under light occlusion and 90% in crowded penalty-box scenes (5+ players), but only 50% recovery from heavy occlusion and — the killer for your panning-camera case — 8.66% re-acquisition success when players leave the frame, concluding such methods "require stronger re-identification mechanisms for extended absences." That is precisely your failure mode at scale (8,695 raw tracklets). (2) Roboflow — whose football stack your pipeline mirrors (RF-DETR detection + ByteTrack/BoT-SORT + SigLIP team clustering in roboflow/sports) — integrated SAM3 at launch but positions it for auto-labeling, annotation acceleration, and PoCs, NOT as a replacement for detector+tracker in sports tracking; their sports tutorials still ship detector+tracker. (3) Community sports pipelines that do use SAM2 (e.g., the Banandre basketball pipeline: RF-DETR + SAM2 + SigLIP) use it for segmentation masks feeding appearance models — not as the identity tracker. Known failure modes relevant to you: crowded-scene matching ambiguity (acknowledged in Meta's own paper — SAM3's video annotation effort deliberately oversampled "clips with many crowded objects and tracking failures"); identical-appearance instances (10 same-kit players) defeat IoU-only masklet matching; per-object latency scaling makes 20+ objects the worst case; small far-side players (~15-25 px at 1080p) are marginal for text-prompted detection at SAM3's ~1008px inference resolution; long-video memory growth forces chunking. Versus your RF-DETR (21.8 fps) + BoT-SORT (76 fps) stack: SAM3 is ~10-40x slower for strictly less identity persistence — the only thing it adds is pixel masks.
complementary_uses: Three candidates, two worth doing: (1) MASK-CLEANED CROPS FOR SIGLIP TEAM EMBEDDINGS — highest ROI. Your 0.479 team-cluster silhouette is depressed partly by grass/background pixels dominating small player crops. Run SAM3 in image mode on sampled keyframes (1 fps sampling = 5,400 frames; 30 ms/frame for 100+ objects on H200 means ~0.3-0.5 s/frame on T4, so ~0.5-0.8 GPU-h/match) using your existing RF-DETR boxes as geometry prompts (cheaper and more reliable than text prompts for tiny far-side players), then embed only foreground pixels (background zeroed or mean-filled). This directly attacks both the team-clustering silhouette and SigLIP tracklet-merge precision — total pipeline cost ~2.0 GPU-h, inside the 3.5 gate. Validate with an A/B on silhouette + merged-tracklet count before committing. (2) PITCH SEGMENTATION replacing the HSV green heuristic — prompt "soccer pitch"/"grass field" once every 1-2 seconds of footage (panning camera changes view slowly); a few thousand image-mode calls/match is negligible cost and is robust where HSV fails (shadows, evening/floodlit matches, worn goalmouth turf, sand-colored pitches, green advertising boards bleeding into the green mask). Cache the pitch mask between samples. (3) JERSEY-NUMBER EXEMPLAR PROMPTING — skip it. Far-side numbers at 1080p are single-digit pixels tall, below SAM3's useful concept-detection scale; an exemplar prompt for "jersey number" will be noise-dominated. Your number-reading problem stays a crop-upscale + OCR problem anchored to torso regions (which the masks from use #1 give you for free, slightly improving torso localization). A fourth, offline-only use: SAM3 text/exemplar prompting as an auto-labeler (Roboflow's recommended use) to cheaply generate segmentation/kit-color training data for a small distilled seg head if image-mode T4 cost ever matters — the SAM License permits training on outputs.
